title: Generative Social Choice: The Next Generation

reviews.summary: The paper extends the “generative social choice” framework to settings with statement costs and a global budget, and to the more realistic case where the underlying LLM-powered queries are only approximate. It proposes a parameterized democratic process that, under approximate discriminative and generative queries, guarantees an approximate form of balanced justified representation adapted to costs ((b, d)-costBJR) with explicit dependence on the error parameters. Complementary lower bounds show these guarantees are nearly tight. The authors also implement a practical system, PROSE, using GPT-4o, and demonstrate improved proportionality and user-utility proxies over several baselines on drug-review and civic deliberation datasets.
strengths: Introduces cost-aware proportional representation with budgets into generative social choice, bridging committee elections and participatory budgeting in an unbounded candidate space.
Formalizes approximate discriminative and generative queries via parameters (β, γ, δ, μ) and derives guarantees that degrade gracefully with these errors.
Provides nearly matching impossibility results, clarifying what is and isn’t achievable under query inaccuracies.
Designs an algorithmic framework with tunable query sets (C, f) that exposes a useful runtime–guarantee trade-off.
Includes a synthetic environment to probe behavior under controlled error models, showing performance well above worst-case bounds.
Implements an end-to-end system (PROSE) and evaluates on two distinct domains with multiple baselines, reporting statistical tests and proportionality violation analyses.
Clear separation between theory (model, axioms, guarantees, lower bounds) and practice (system design, datasets, baselines).
The impact of each error source on the guarantee is explained intuitively in addition to the formal theorems.
Advances the theoretical underpinnings of AI-assisted democratic processes in realistic, budgeted settings with imperfect models.
Provides a practical engine potentially useful for civic tech and participatory platforms that must summarize diverse opinions proportionally within length constraints.
weaknesses: The core evaluation of proportionality violations samples witness statements only from statements generated during PROSE runs; this can underestimate violations for some methods and introduces a method-dependent sampling bias.
Proposition 3.5 requires knowledge of μ and γ; while the main algorithm is agnostic, the tightest unit-cost result depends on parameters that are difficult to estimate in practice.
The assumption that utilities are effectively additive and that the universe is “closed under union” (Appendix A) is strong for natural language and may not always hold empirically.
Evaluation relies on a separate GPT-4o CoT discriminative proxy, remaining within a single model family; while correlation is reported low, shared priors can still bias outcomes.
Agent populations are small (41–80), and certain datasets are rebalanced; real-world skewed distributions (which matter for proportionality) are underexplored.

Baseline set lacks established (non-LLM) budgeted/proportional summarization or fair clustering/summarization methods (e.g., submodular summarization with fairness constraints), and lacks comparisons to non-GPT LLMs to test robustness.
Minor notation/rendering issues (e.g., brackets around μx in Eq. (1), typesetting artifacts in Theorem 3.4) hinder smooth reading, though not the substance.
Some naming inconsistencies (e.g., “Birth Control (Balanced/Uniform)”) and occasional reliance on footnotes for key intuitions could be tightened.

Limited discussion of connections to fairness-aware summarization, diversity-aware submodular selection, or budgeted text summarization literature that also tackles proportionality/diversity under length constraints.

Practical links to participatory budgeting algorithmics beyond theoretical alignment (e.g., comparisons to sequential-Phragmén-inspired query procedures) are suggested in Discussion but not empirically explored.
reviews.questions:
1. Proportionality violation measurement: You sample potential witness statements from PROSE’s generated pool. How would the results change if witness statements were sampled (or generated) per method, or via a method-agnostic generator? Can you report sensitivity analyses?
2. Error parameters: Have you attempted to empirically estimate β, γ, δ, μ for your implementation (e.g., via held-out calibration tasks)? Even rough ranges would contextualize the theoretical bounds vis-à-vis practice.
3. Additive utility and union-closure: In natural language, concatenation can degrade clarity and utility nonlinearly. Can you provide empirical evidence (e.g., ablations) supporting approximate additivity in your domains?
4. Baselines: Could you compare against non-LLM budgeted/fair summarization or diversity-aware submodular selection methods under the same length budgets? This would better situate PROSE’s gains beyond LLM-specific pipelines.
5. Cross-model robustness: Did you try evaluating with a different LLM family (or smaller open models) as the CoT judge, or perform limited human evaluation, to mitigate model-family bias?
6. Scalability: Complex-DemocraticProcess requires extensive querying over C=[B] and multiple ℓ. What empirical heuristics (e.g., pruning, bandits) proved most effective, and how do they affect proportionality in practice?
7. Bowling Green curation: How were agents/comments selected to ensure “non-trivial overlap”? Could this selection bias inflate measured proportionality? Please clarify inclusion criteria.
reviews.assessment: This paper makes a substantive and timely contribution by (i) extending generative social choice to cost/budgeted settings, (ii) rigorously modeling approximate query responses and deriving nearly tight proportionality guarantees, and (iii) delivering a practical system that performs well on multiple datasets. The theoretical development is clear and strong, with well-motivated definitions and sharp lower bounds. The empirical section shows promise but would benefit from stronger, more model-agnostic evaluation protocols (e.g., broader baselines, cross-model or human assessments) and from addressing potential sampling biases in proportionality violation checks. Overall, I view this as a high-quality submission that advances the state of the art in AI-assisted democratic decision support and recommend acceptance, with suggested clarifications and additional experiments to further solidify the empirical claims.
